era_context: Late Bronze Age; Egypt under Amenhotep III in the final years of his reign (death c. 1370 BCE). The Hittite Empire under Šuppiluliuma I was at the height of its power. Mycenaean Greece was in Late Helladic IIIA. The Shang Dynasty in China continued. This was an era of imperial consolidation, international diplomacy, and military competition.

events.title: Amenhotep III's reign in final years; possible regnal year 36–37
events.description: Amenhotep III was in the final years of his reign, which lasted approximately 38–39 years (c. 1390–1352 BCE, depending on chronological framework). The pharaoh's health was likely declining, though contemporary sources do not explicitly document this. His son, the future Akhenaten (then known as Amenhotep IV), may have begun co-regency or been designated as successor, though the evidence for co-regency is debated among Egyptologists.
key_figures: Amenhotep IV / Akhenaten (future pharaoh, possibly co-regent)

sources.name: Historiographic consensus on Amenhotep III's chronology

events.certainty_note: Amenhotep III's reign is well-attested, but the precise dating of his death and the nature of any co-regency with Akhenaten are debated. Chronological frameworks vary by ±5–10 years.

events.title: Šuppiluliuma I's dominance in Syria and Anatolia
events.description: Šuppiluliuma I had consolidated Hittite control over much of Syria and Anatolia by this point in his reign. The Hittite Empire was at its territorial peak, with vassal states established throughout the region. This dominance was achieved through military campaigns and diplomatic arrangements, as documented in Hittite royal annals.
key_figures: Šuppiluliuma I (Great King of the Hittites)

sources.name: Hittite royal annals (cuneiform tablets from Boğazköy)

events.certainty_note: Šuppiluliuma I's reign and major achievements are well-documented, but precise year-by-year dating is uncertain. Chronological frameworks place his major campaigns in the 1340s–1330s BCE, with some variation.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Amenhotep III's death and the nature of any co-regency with Akhenaten are highly contested among Egyptologists. Some scholars argue for a long co-regency (10+ years), while others argue for little or no co-regency. This affects the precise dating of events in this period. Additionally, the chronology of the Late Bronze Age remains one of the most debated issues in ancient history, with competing frameworks differing by up to 15 years.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1372 BCE is dependent on Egyptian and Hittite royal inscriptions and administrative records. The bias toward court-sponsored narratives means that perspectives from smaller polities and non-elite populations are largely absent. The Amarna Letters, a crucial source for this era, were compiled during Akhenaten's reign and do not directly document 1372 BCE. Archaeological evidence provides some independent verification but lacks year-level precision. The chronology of the Late Bronze Age remains contested.

graph_edges.note: Amenhotep III's death and succession of Akhenaten
